Using Call-In Stubs

Using Call-In Stubs

You can generate call-in stub code for a 4GL service rather than doing this manually.

In the example for uecreate, code is included to instantiate and activate the 4GL operation. For an operation without parameters, this is only a few lines and easily coded by hand. However, if the operation has parameters, code to set and get parameters must be added, making the process tedious and error-prone.

Uniface provides a command to create this C code automatically, using the data from the compiled signature descriptor. This created C code is usually referred to as ‘call-in stub code’. This code enables you to activate a 4GL service by simply calling a C function (the so-called ‘stub function’). The stub function hides all the Uniface-specific details of the service, operation, parameters, and activation.

To create call-in stub code for a 4GL service, compile your service and update the UAR file.

Then issue the command ide /sti ServiceName. This will create the files ui_servicename.c and ui_servicename.h.

If you look at the C source, you see an #include of the header file, and generated stub functions for all operations in the service (including the ACCEPT, EXEC, and QUIT operations, which are defined by default).

For example, if you have a service CALLINSRV with one operation CALLIN, you will see the following stub functions in ui_callinsrv.c:

long ui_accept_callinsrv ()
long ui_callin_callinsrv ()
long ui_exec_callinsrv ()
long ui_quit_callinsrv ()

You can now activate operation CALLIN by simply calling the ui_callin_callinsrv function.

Without using call-in stubs, your call-in program that activates operation CALLIN would look like this:

#include <h3gl.h>
void main (void)
{
UHACT hEnv; /* Uniface environment */
UHACT hIn; /* Instance handle */
UHACT hOp; /* Operation handle */
long opStat; /* Activate status return parameter */
uecreate(1, 0, 0, "my.asn", 0, 0, &hEnv); /* Create the environment */
uinstnew(hEnv, "CALLINSRV", 0, 0, /* Instantiate service CALLINSRV */
UDEFAULT_COMM_MODE, 0, &hIn, 0);
uinstopr(hIn, "CALLIN", 0, &hOp); /* Instantiate the CALLIN operation */
uopract (hOp, &opStat, 0); /* Activate the operation */
uinstdel(hIn, 0); /* Destroy the instance */
uedelete(hEnv); /* Shut down the environment */
ufreeh (&hOp); /* Free the operation */
ufreeh (&hIn); /* Free the instance */
ufreeh (&hEnv); /* Free the environment handle */
}

Using the generated call-in stub code, the same program looks like this:

#include <h3gl.h>
#include <ui_callinsrv.c>
void main (void)
{
UHACT hEnv; /* Uniface environment */
uecreate(1, 0, 0, "my.asn", 0, 0, &hEnv); /* Create the environment */
ui_callin_callinsrv(); /* Instantiate and activate */
uedelete(hEnv); /* Shut down the environment */
ufreeh (&hEnv); /* Free the environment handle */
}

This is already much shorter and easier, and we are not even using parameters yet. The only thing you must do, except for calling the stub function, is to call uecreate at the beginning and uedelete at the end of the program.

In this example we have the main source code #include of the generated stub file. Alternatively, you could compile this file separately and link it into the program, along with the compiled code from the main source, as you will.

Note that generated stub code is not meant to be edited by the user. Of course, it is quite possible to do so, and it is not necessarily a problem if you do, but you are not eligible for support in case the modified code does not work as expected.
